description: The rate and rhythm at which an activity, exchange, or sequence proceeds. Pace may change within one event and may be too fast, too slow, or uneven for a stated participant and purpose. 'thimu' (time) names the broader dimension; 'thimelo' names how action is distributed through it.
articulatory_notes: Begin with unvoiced dental 'th', keeping the tongue tip light against the upper teeth as air passes into high-front unrounded 'i'. Close the lips for voiced nasal 'm', carry the sound through the nose, and open into stressed true-mid front unrounded 'e'. The tongue tip returns to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l' before the word settles into true-mid back rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The lips close around the stressed middle, briefly interrupting the airflow before the final lateral opens into round 'o'. That small pause and release can recall a cadence changing under attention.
usage_notes: Pace is often modified by a speed or comparison word. A possessor may name the meeting, task, speech, route, or other activity whose pace is under discussion. 'thimora' names the planned arrangement of activities, while 'lumethi' names a claimed latest time.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Attention has a pace. Forcing every perception or decision into the same interval can hide what a less hurried notice would reveal.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Naming pace makes speed negotiable instead of treating the fastest speaker or process as everyone's natural clock.

semantic_domains.activity: rate and rhythm of a continuing action
semantic_domains.communication: speed at which an exchange proceeds
